blocks.content: By five o'clock, the sky to the west had started doing something.
Not the usual late-afternoon purple over the tree line. Something more organized — a gray-green line building up low on the horizon, the kind that made old people go inside and check their windows.
Mr. Whitaker had said he was praying for rain.

The kids at the diamond had been trickling away for twenty minutes — parents calling, the sky doing something worth paying attention to. Anna had gone home at four-thirty, then the Lesters, leaving just the four of them and Tommy, who was looking at the western horizon with focused uncertainty.
Tommy went. They gathered their things. The game score was not formally concluded, which meant Daisy won by default. She didn't say so.
The wind had picked up the way it does before a Southern summer storm — a warm front that was somehow both a relief and a warning. It moved the tops of the oak trees in a slow wave, south to north.
Kit was still looking at the sky. The way the green-gray was spreading. The way the clouds were building fast in a direction they hadn't been building twenty minutes ago.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Might not hit for a while yet.
Daisy put her hand on her hip.
